summary: I'm originally from Louisville, Kentucky, where I was born and raised. That's where my parents started mass producing ceramic pitcher and bowl sets back in the late 70s and early 80s. I began selling merchandise at flea markets and festivals around Kentucky, including Louisville, when I was about 14. Louisville is really the place where my entrepreneurial journey began.

I used to sell at events organized by Stewart's Promotions, which held monthly fairs at places like the fairgrounds and Rupp Arena. They were a big part of the flea market scene in Kentucky when I was starting out. Stewart's Promotions helped me get exposure to a wider audience and build my business. It was one of the main venues where I sold merchandise in my early years.

Rupp Arena was one of the locations where Stewart's Promotions held monthly flea markets that I sold at. It was a popular spot in Kentucky for these events, and I got to set up there regularly. Selling at Rupp Arena helped me reach a lot of customers and grow my merchandise sales. It was part of my early selling circuit in the 1980s.

I also sold at smaller markets in places like Shepherdsville, Kentucky. These local markets were part of my weekend selling routine when I was a teenager. Shepherdsville was one of the towns where I could take the merchandise I got from my dad's trades and sell directly to customers. It was a good way to build experience and make sales outside of bigger venues.

Elizabethtown, Kentucky was another place where I sold merchandise at flea markets and festivals. Like Shepherdsville, it was part of my early selling circuit around the state. I traveled to these towns regularly to sell the goods I acquired through my dad's trading business. It helped me get familiar with different markets and customers in Kentucky.

When the cold months came in Kentucky, I would travel down to Florida to sell during the winter. Starting around age 19, I spent three to four months there each year to avoid the cold and keep my business going. Florida offered a warmer climate and different crowds, which was great for my seasonal selling. I always returned to Kentucky when the weather warmed up again.

I've traveled and sold merchandise in about 13 or 14 states across the United States, mostly on the East Coast. I've been to Texas and other places, but my main focus has been the eastern part of the country. Traveling different parts of the United States has been exciting because each region has its own lifestyle and people. I really enjoy seeing different places and meeting a variety of folks through my work.

I've sold merchandise in Texas as part of my travels, although most of my selling has been on the East Coast. Texas was one of the few western states I visited for festivals and markets. It was interesting to experience the different culture and market there compared to the East Coast. But overall, Texas was just one stop among many in my selling journey across the country.

I started coming to Court Day in 1982 when I was 17, after a friend of my dad's, John Smith from Mount Sterling, invited me to join him. I set up on Queen Street, the same spot I use now, and have been coming every year since. Court Day was a friendly and welcoming event, and it became a big part of my selling calendar. It's where I met many people, including my future wife, and really grew my business over the decades.

Mount Sterling, Kentucky is where Court Day takes place, and it's been a key location for me since 1982. I first came there with John Smith, a friend of my dad's who was from Mount Sterling. I've set up my merchandise on Queen Street in Mount Sterling every year since then. The town and its community have been central to my selling career and personal life.

John Smith was a friend of my dad's from Mount Sterling who invited me to come to Court Day in 1982 when I was 17. He was in his 50s at the time and had room in his trailer for me to bring my merchandise. I rented space next to him for a couple of years before getting my own spots. John was important in helping me get started at Court Day and was a good vendor to work alongside.

I heard a lot about Shipshewana, Indiana from other vendors and was curious to see what it was like. It's a well-known market in the northern part of Indiana. I made it a point to go there at least once to experience it firsthand. We used to go there regularly but haven't been in about ten years now.

I came up to Fort Knox for a big two-week tent sale after my uncle told me about it. That was when I met my wife, through mutual friends connected to that event. I ended up staying in Kentucky for four months straight after that sale. Fort Knox was a significant place in my life because it led to meeting my wife and deepening my ties to Kentucky.

My wife is originally from Wilmington, North Carolina, though she considers herself from Kentucky now. We met in Kentucky, but her roots are in Wilmington. It's interesting because even though she was born there, she identifies more with Kentucky after all the time we've spent here together.

Labor Day marks the start of my busy selling season, usually beginning around the first of September. I kick off the season at a big festival in Hillsville, Virginia, which lasts four days and draws about 2.5 million people. Labor Day is a key event for me because summer is slow, and the fall festivals really pick up business. It's a great time to get back on the road and sell outdoors.

Memorial Day is part of the festival schedule in Hillsville, Virginia, although the Labor Day event there is bigger. We do festivals from Labor Day through Memorial Day, which is the busiest time for selling. Memorial Day marks the end of the season and is a time when many outdoor markets and festivals happen. It helps bookend the selling calendar for me.

Hillsville, Virginia is where I start my selling season each year on Labor Day. The festival there lasts four days and draws a huge crowd, about 2.5 million people, with many arriving by tour bus. I've been going to that festival since 1998, and it's one of the biggest events I do outside of Court Day. Hillsville is a lively place that really kicks off the fall selling season for me.

McDonald's didn't come up directly in my story, so I don't have much to say about it from my experience. My focus has been on flea markets, festivals, and outdoor selling events rather than fast food or restaurant chains. So McDonald's hasn't played a role in my selling career or personal history as far as I recall.
cues: focus on flea markets and festivals
